title: Your first struct
successMessage: Structs group related data. alice.name accesses the member. Default initialization with {"Alice", 30, 1.68} uses the order fields are declared. You can mix designated initializers in C++20: Person{.name="Alice", .age=30}.

title: Structs with methods
hint: string greet() { return "Hi, I'm " + name; } bool isAdult() { return age >= 18; }
successMessage: Structs with methods — this is where C++ structs and classes blur. The only difference: struct members are public by default. Use structs for simple data containers; classes for complex objects with invariants.

title: enum class — type-safe enumerations
instruction:
Old-style `enum` pollutes the namespace and converts to int implicitly — dangerous.
`enum class` (C++11) is scoped and type-safe:

```cpp
enum class Direction { North, South, East, West };
Direction d = Direction::North;  // must use scope
```
successMessage: enum class forces you to write Status::Active, not just Active — preventing name collisions. You can't accidentally compare Status to Priority. This is a huge improvement over C-style enums.

title: Structs as function parameters
instruction:
Pass structs by **const reference** to avoid copying:

```cpp
void printInfo(const Person& p) {  // no copy!
    cout << p.name << endl;
}
```

Return structs by value — the compiler optimises this with RVO (Return Value Optimisation).
successMessage: 6-8-10 right triangle — distance is 10. midpoint is (3, 4). Return by value is zero-cost thanks to Return Value Optimisation — the compiler builds the result directly in the caller's memory.

title: Nested structs
instruction: Structs can contain other structs — building complex data models.
successMessage: Nested structs build hierarchical data models. emp.address.city accesses the nested field. This is how database rows, API responses, and game entities are represented.

title: Build a playing card system
instruction:
No starter code.

Build:
- `enum class Suit { Clubs, Diamonds, Hearts, Spades }`
- `enum class Rank { Two=2, Three, ..., Ten, Jack, Queen, King, Ace }`
- `struct Card { Suit suit; Rank rank; string toString(); }`
- `toString()` returns e.g. `"King of Hearts"`

Create 5 cards and print them.
hint: Use switch in toString() to convert enum values to strings.

codeChecks.message: Add a toString() method to Card.

successMessage: A type-safe playing card system. enum class Suit prevents Suit::Hearts == Rank::Ace comparisons. toString with switch converts enums to readable strings. Chapter 11 done!
